Handling mini batching.
So what do we have to do when we're handling mini batching?
If we were handling mini batching in feed forward networks, it's actually relatively easy.
Because we all of our computations are the same shape.
So we just can catenate them all together into a big tensor and run run over it.
We saw mini batching makes things much faster, but mini batching and sequence modeling is harder than in feed forward networks.
One reason is in our lens, each word depends on the previous word.
Also because sequences are various links.
So what we do to handle this is we do padding and masking.
So we can do padding like this.
So we just add an extra token at the end to make all of the sequences at the same length.
If we are doing an encoder decoder style model, where we have an input.
And then we want to generate all the outputs based on the input.
One of the easy things is to add pads to the beginning.
And then so yeah, it doesn't really matter, but you can add pads to the beginning.
So they're all starting at the same place, especially if you're using RNN style models.
Then we calculate the loss over the output, for example, we multiply the loss by a mask to remove the loss over the token set we don't care about.
And we take the sum of these.
And so luckily, most of this is implemented in, for example, pytorch or a high-con case transformers already.
So you don't need to worry about it, but it is a good idea to know what's going on under the hood.
If you want to implement anything unusual.
And also, it's good to know for the following reason also, which is bucketing and sorting.
So if we use sentences of vastly different lengths and we put them in the same mini batch, this can waste a really large amount of computation.
So like, let's say we're processing documents or movie reviews or something like that.
And you have a most movie reviews are like 10 words long, but you have one movie review in your mini batch of a thousand words.
So basically what that means is you're padding most of your sequences, 990 times, the process 10 sequences, which is like a lot of waste, right?
Because you're running them all for your GPU and other things like that.
So one way to remedy this is to sort sentences.
So similarly, link sentences are in the same batch.
So you, you first sort before building all of your batches and then that makes it so that similarly size ones are in the same batch.
